content:
# Joshua Chapter 7 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), folio pages 230-231 | Codex Vaticanus (LXX)
**Source Image:** `JOS_007_page_001.jpg`, `JOS_007_page_002.jpg`

---

**7:1** But the sons of Israel committed a trespass regarding the devoted thing, for Achan the son of Carmi, the son of Zabdi, the son of Zerah, of the tribe of Judah, took from the devoted thing. And the anger of YHWH burned against the sons of Israel.

**7:2** And Joshua sent men from Jericho to the Ai, which is near Beth-aven, east of Bethel, and he said to them, saying, "Go up and spy out the land." And the men went up and spied out the Ai.

**7:3** And they returned to Joshua and said to him, "Let not all the people go up. Let about two thousand men or about three thousand men go up and strike the Ai. Do not weary all the people there, for they are few."

**7:4** So about three thousand men from the people went up there, and they fled before the men of the Ai.

**7:5** And the men of the Ai struck down from them about thirty-six men, and they pursued them from before the gate as far as the Shebarim, and struck them down on the descent. And the heart of the people melted and became as water.

---

**7:6** And Joshua tore his garments and fell on his face to the earth before the ark of YHWH until the evening, he and the elders of Israel, and they put dust upon their heads.

**7:7** And Joshua said, "Alas, my Lord YHWH, why have You brought bringing this people across the Jordan, to give us into the hand of the Amorite, to destroy us? And if only we had been willing and had dwelt beyond the Jordan!

**7:8** "Please, my Lord, what shall I say, after Israel has turned its back before its enemies?

**7:9** "And the Canaanites and all the inhabitants of the land will hear, and they will surround us, and they will cut off our name from the earth. And what will You do for Your great name?"

---

**7:10** And YHWH said to Joshua, "Rise! Why is it that you have fallen on your face?

**7:11** "Israel has sinned, and they have also transgressed My covenant which I commanded them, and they have also taken from the devoted thing, and have also stolen, and have also deceived, and have also put it among their own vessels.

**7:12** "And the sons of Israel are not able to stand before their enemies; they turn their backs before their enemies, for they have become a devoted thing. I will not continue to be with you, unless you destroy the devoted thing from your midst.

**7:13** "Rise, consecrate the people, and say, 'Consecrate yourselves for tomorrow, for thus says YHWH, the God of Israel: A devoted thing is in your midst, O Israel. You are not able to stand before your enemies until you remove the devoted thing from your midst.

**7:14** "'And in the morning you shall come near by your tribes. And it shall be that the tribe which YHWH takes shall come near by clans. And the clan which YHWH takes shall come near by households. And the household which YHWH takes shall come near man by man.

**7:15** "'And it shall be that the one taken with the devoted thing shall be burned with fire, he and all that belongs to him, because he transgressed the covenant of YHWH, and because he committed a disgraceful thing in Israel.'"

---

**7:16** And Joshua rose early in the morning and brought Israel near by its tribes, and the tribe of Judah was taken.

**7:17** And he brought near the clan of Judah, and he took the clan of the Zerahites. And he brought near the clan of the Zerahites man by man, and Zabdi was taken.

**7:18** And he brought near his household man by man, and Achan the son of Carmi, the son of Zabdi, the son of Zerah, of the tribe of Judah, was taken.

**7:19** And Joshua said to Achan, "My son, give, I pray, glory to YHWH, the God of Israel, and give to Him a confession. Tell me, I pray, what you have done; do not hide it from me."

**7:20** And Achan answered Joshua and said, "Truly, I have sinned against YHWH, the God of Israel, and this is what I have done:

**7:21** "I saw among the spoil one beautiful mantle of Shinar, and two hundred shekels of silver, and one tongue of gold -- fifty shekels its weight -- and I coveted them, and I took them. And behold, they are hidden in the earth in the midst of my tent, with the silver underneath it."

**7:22** And Joshua sent messengers, and they ran to the tent, and behold, it was hidden in his tent, with the silver underneath it.

**7:23** And they took them from the midst of the tent and brought them to Joshua and to all the sons of Israel, and they spread them out before YHWH.

**7:24** And Joshua -- and all Israel with him -- took Achan the son of Zerah, and the silver, and the mantle, and the tongue of gold, and his sons, and his daughters, and his ox, and his donkey, and his flock, and his tent, and all that belonged to him, and they brought them up to the Valley of Achor.

**7:25** And Joshua said, "Why have you troubled us? YHWH will trouble you this day." And all Israel stoned him with stones, and they burned them with fire, and they stoned them with stones.

**7:26** And they raised over him a great heap of stones, unto this day. And YHWH turned from the burning of His anger. Therefore the name of that place has been called the Valley of Achor unto this day.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Manuscript Observation -- Leningrad Codex (B19A), Pages 230-231

Both manuscript folios were examined directly. The first image (JOS_007_page_001.jpg, page 230) presents the Leningrad Codex in its characteristic three-column layout with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks visible throughout. The folio contains the end of Joshua 6 transitioning into Joshua 7 and runs through approximately verse 12-13. The divine name יְהוָה is clearly visible in multiple instances with its standard Masoretic notation, including the critical occurrences in verses 1 (YHWH's anger burning), 6 (before the ark of YHWH), 7 (Adonai YHWH in Joshua's lament), and 10 (YHWH's response). The genealogy of Achan in verse 1 -- עָכָן בֶּן־כַּרְמִי בֶן־זַבְדִּי בֶן־זֶרַח -- is legible in the opening section. The consonantal text confirms the MT reading throughout.

The second image (JOS_007_page_002.jpg, page 231) continues from approximately verse 13 through verse 26 and into the opening of Joshua 8. The critical narrowing-selection sequence of verses 16-18, the confession of Achan in verses 20-21, and the execution narrative of verses 24-26 are visible across the columns. The parashah markers are visible at the major structural seams. The shift from singular to plural in verse 25 -- from אֹתוֹ to אֹתָם -- is confirmed in the manuscript. No anomalous readings were observed that would require departing from the standard MT tradition.

The Vaticanus LXX image (JOS_007_vaticanus_page_001.jpg, page 0170) shows the Greek uncial text in its characteristic three-column layout. The chapter heading marker is visible, and the Greek text of Joshua 7 (designated as chapter 19 in the Vaticanus pagination context) is legible. The reading Αχαρ (Achar) rather than the MT's Achan is visible, as is the expanded genealogy. The LXX variants noted by all three agents -- including the addition of μεγαλην ("great") to the trespass in verse 1 and the treatment of Shebarim as a verb in verse 5 -- are consistent with the Vaticanus tradition as observed.

---

### Verse-by-Verse Arbitration

#### 7:1 -- The trespass; cognate accusative; genealogy; corporate guilt

**Cognate accusative (וַיִּמְעֲלוּ...מַעַל):** EMET preserves the literal cognate accusative "trespassed a trespass." LOGOS renders "committed a trespass," which preserves the sacral weight of the root ma'al without the surface awkwardness. PNEUMA renders "broke faith with what had been set apart for destruction," which communicates the relational and sacral dimensions dynamically. **Consensus adopts "committed a trespass" (LOGOS)** -- it preserves the technical sacral terminology of ma'al (a breach of faith with what is holy, not ordinary wrongdoing) while reading naturally in English. EMET's doubled construction, while faithful to the Hebrew surface form, reads as stilted English prose in a narrative context. PNEUMA's rendering is accurate in meaning but loses the technical term "trespass" which carries weight throughout the chapter.

**"But" vs. "And":** PNEUMA opens with "But"; EMET opens with "And-trespassed"; LOGOS opens with "But." The Hebrew waw-consecutive (וַיִּמְעֲלוּ) functions here as an adversative conjunction -- the transition from the triumph of Jericho (chapter 6) to the sin of Achan is a sharp reversal. **Consensus adopts "But"** (LOGOS, PNEUMA) to mark this narrative turn.

**Genealogy -- "Achan" vs. "Achar":** All three agents follow the MT reading עָכָן (Achan) over the LXX Αχαρ (Achar). The Vaticanus reading Achar is also the form in 1 Chronicles 2:7 and connects more directly to the verb 'akhar ("to trouble") and the Valley of Achor. **Consensus follows the MT: Achan.** The wordplay with the valley name is noted in the council notes but does not override the Leningrad Codex reading.

**Corporate guilt:** EMET makes the sons of Israel the explicit subject of the trespass, with Achan as a secondary specification. LOGOS restructures with "for Achan...took," making the individual act the cause of the corporate charge. PNEUMA follows LOGOS's structure. **Consensus adopts LOGOS's structure** -- "the sons of Israel committed a trespass...for Achan...took" -- which preserves both the corporate charge and its individual cause, with the causal "for" making the logic explicit.

#### 7:2 -- "The Ai" (ha-'Ai); topographical references

**The definite article with Ai:** EMET preserves "the-Ai" throughout, reflecting the Hebrew הָעַי. LOGOS drops the article, following English convention. PNEUMA drops the article. **Consensus retains "the Ai"** (following EMET) -- the Hebrew consistently uses the definite article because the name means "the ruin," and this descriptive force is part of the narrative's irony (Israel is ruined at "the Ruin"). The article is grammatically natural in English when explained as a descriptive designation, and its preservation maintains a distinctive feature of the Hebrew text that all three agents acknowledge in their notes.

**"Said to them, saying":** EMET preserves the Hebrew pleonasm (וַיֹּאמֶר...לֵאמֹר) as "said to them, saying." LOGOS renders "he spoke to them, saying." PNEUMA condenses to "told them." **Consensus adopts "he said to them, saying"** -- preserving the Hebrew conjunction style (לֵאמֹר as the standard speech-introduction formula) per the project rules on Hebrew conjunction style. The pleonasm is characteristic of Biblical Hebrew narrative and should not be suppressed.

**"Spy out" vs. "scout":** EMET and LOGOS use "spy out" (וַיְרַגְּלוּ); PNEUMA uses "scout." **Consensus adopts "spy out"** (EMET, LOGOS) -- the verb רגל carries the covert intelligence-gathering sense that "spy" preserves and "scout" softens.

#### 7:3 -- The scouts' report; numbers

**Numbers:** All three agents render "about two thousand men or about three thousand men" with English words. **Consensus follows this.** The numbers are spelled out per project rules.

**"Do not weary" vs. "Don't exhaust":** EMET and LOGOS use "weary" (אַל־תְּיַגַּע); PNEUMA uses "exhaust." **Consensus adopts "Do not weary all the people there"** (EMET/LOGOS) -- the Hiphil of יגע means to cause weariness or toil, and "weary" is the closer English equivalent.

#### 7:4 -- "So" vs. "And"

LOGOS opens with "So" (interpreting the waw as consequential); EMET with "And-went-up"; PNEUMA with "So." **Consensus adopts "So"** -- the verse narrates the consequence of the scouts' advice, and the consequential "So" marks this connection clearly.

#### 7:5 -- "The Shebarim"; the heart-melting reversal

**"The Shebarim":** EMET preserves the article: "the-Shebarim." LOGOS uses "Shebarim" without the article. PNEUMA uses "Shebarim." **Consensus retains "the Shebarim"** (following EMET) for the same reason as "the Ai" -- the Hebrew uses the article (הַשְּׁבָרִים), and the word may be a common noun ("the broken places") rather than a proper name. Preserving the article keeps both readings available.

**"Melted and became as water":** All three agents preserve the visceral image of the heart melting. EMET: "melted...and-it-became as-water"; LOGOS: "melted and became as water"; PNEUMA: "melted and turned to water." **Consensus adopts "melted and became as water"** (LOGOS) -- "became as water" preserves the comparative כ (as) that the Hebrew uses, where PNEUMA's "turned to water" implies a complete transformation rather than a simile.

#### 7:6 -- Joshua's prostration; "dust upon their heads"

All three agents agree in substance. **"Dust upon their heads"** (EMET, with the preposition "upon" matching the Hebrew עַל) is adopted over LOGOS/PNEUMA's "dust on their heads" -- "upon" is the more formal register appropriate to the gesture of mourning. The phrase "fell on his face to the earth" is unanimous.

#### 7:7 -- Joshua's lament; infinitive absolute; "Alas, my Lord YHWH"

**"Alas" (אֲהָהּ):** EMET renders "Alas"; LOGOS renders "Alas"; PNEUMA renders "Oh no." **Consensus adopts "Alas"** (EMET, LOGOS) -- the interjection אֲהָהּ is a solemn anguished lament form used in prophetic address (Jeremiah 1:6; 4:10; Ezekiel 4:14; 9:8). "Alas" preserves this formal register. "Oh no" (PNEUMA) domesticates the expression to casual speech.

**Infinitive absolute (הַעֲבִיר הֶעֱבַרְתָּ):** EMET renders "bring bringing" per its doubling rule. LOGOS renders "brought...at all." PNEUMA renders "bring...at all." The Hebrew employs the Hiphil infinitive absolute + finite verb for emphasis. **Consensus adopts the doubled infinitive absolute construction: "brought bringing"** (following EMET's pattern and the project rules on infinitive absolute with doubling). The rendering becomes "why have You brought bringing this people across the Jordan" -- preserving the emphatic Hebrew construction per the explicit project rules.

**"My Lord YHWH" (אֲדֹנָי יְהוִה):** EMET: "my-Lord YHWH"; LOGOS: "Lord YHWH" (without possessive); PNEUMA: "Lord YHWH." The Hebrew is אֲדֹנָי with the first-person possessive suffix: literally "my lords" (pluralis maiestatis). **Consensus adopts "my Lord YHWH"** (following EMET) -- preserving the possessive that the Hebrew includes.

**"If only we had been willing and had dwelt beyond the Jordan":** EMET: "And-if-only we-had-been-willing and-we-had-dwelt on-the-other-side of-the-Jordan!" LOGOS: "Would that we had been content to dwell beyond the Jordan!" PNEUMA: "If only we had been willing to stay on the other side of the Jordan!" The Hebrew has two verbs: הוֹאַלְנוּ וַנֵּשֶׁב -- "we had been willing" and "we had dwelt." **Consensus preserves both verbs** rather than collapsing them: "And if only we had been willing and had dwelt beyond the Jordan!" -- the two verbs express the compound wish (willingness + actual remaining), and collapsing them loses the nuance that Joshua wishes both the decision and the action.

#### 7:8 -- Continued lament

All three agents agree in substance. **Consensus adopts LOGOS's rendering** with minor adjustment: "Please, my Lord, what shall I say, after Israel has turned its back before its enemies?" The possessive "my Lord" follows the convention established at verse 7.

#### 7:9 -- Name theology; "Your great name"

**Word order -- "Your great name" (שִׁמְךָ הַגָּדוֹל):** EMET preserves Hebrew word order: "Your-name the-great." LOGOS: "your great name." PNEUMA: "your great name." **Consensus adopts "Your great name"** (LOGOS, PNEUMA) -- standard English attributive order. The Hebrew places the adjective after the noun in attributive position, which is normal Hebrew syntax, not a marked word order worth preserving in English.

**"They will surround us":** EMET: "they-will-surround upon-us"; LOGOS: "will surround us"; PNEUMA: "they will close in on us from every side." **Consensus adopts "they will surround us"** (LOGOS) -- clean, accurate, preserving the military threat. PNEUMA's expansion "from every side" is interpretive (not in the Hebrew).

#### 7:10 -- YHWH's command to rise

**"Rise!" vs. "Get up!":** EMET: "Rise to-you!"; LOGOS: "Rise up!"; PNEUMA: "Get up!" The Hebrew is קֻם (qum), the imperative of קוּם. **Consensus adopts "Rise!"** (closest to EMET/LOGOS) -- the imperative קוּם is the standard biblical command form; "Rise" preserves its formal weight. "Get up" (PNEUMA) is colloquial. The לְךָ ("to you / for yourself") is an ethical dative adding urgency; **consensus renders simply "Rise!"** -- the urgency is carried by the exclamation and context rather than by adding "to you" (EMET) or "up" (LOGOS), neither of which fully captures the ethical dative.

#### 7:11 -- The fivefold "and also" (וְגַם); covenant accusation

**Fivefold vegam:** EMET preserves all five "and-also" conjunctions. LOGOS preserves "and also" throughout. PNEUMA breaks these into short punching sentences, dropping the "and also" chain. **Consensus preserves the fivefold "and also"** (EMET, LOGOS) -- the rhetorical accumulation of וְגַם is a deliberate stacking device creating mounting outrage. The repetition sounds unusual in English, but that is the point: YHWH's accusation is unusual. The chain of "and also...and also...and also" forces the reader to feel the weight of each successive layer of guilt, which is precisely the Hebrew's effect. PNEUMA's broken sentences are effective English rhetoric but sacrifice the connective tissue that binds the offenses together as a single compound charge.

**"Their own vessels" vs. "their own belongings":** EMET: "in-their-vessels" (בִּכְלֵיהֶם); LOGOS: "among their own vessels"; PNEUMA: "among their own belongings." The Hebrew כְּלִי means "vessel, implement, container." **Consensus adopts "among their own vessels"** (LOGOS) -- preserving the concrete Hebrew noun. "Belongings" (PNEUMA) is a generalizing interpretation.

#### 7:12 -- "They have become a devoted thing"; theological crux

**"Become a devoted thing" vs. "become contaminated":** EMET: "they-have-become devoted-[to-destruction]"; LOGOS: "they have become a devoted thing"; PNEUMA: "they themselves have become contaminated by the devoted thing." The Hebrew is כִּי הָיוּ לְחֵרֶם -- literally "for they have become cherem." LOGOS correctly identifies this as the theological crux of the chapter: Israel has become the very thing that was to be destroyed. **Consensus adopts "for they have become a devoted thing"** (LOGOS) -- this preserves the stark identification (Israel = cherem) that the Hebrew makes. PNEUMA's "contaminated" is interpretively accurate (the cherem functions as contagion) but softens the terrifying directness of the Hebrew declaration: they have not merely been affected by the cherem; they have become it.

**"I will not continue to be with you" vs. "I will not be with you anymore":** EMET: "I-will-not continue to-be with-you"; LOGOS: "I will not be with you anymore"; PNEUMA: "I will not be with you anymore." The Hebrew לֹא אוֹסִיף לִהְיוֹת עִמָּכֶם uses the verb יסף (to add/continue) + infinitive. **Consensus adopts "I will not continue to be with you"** (following EMET's structure) -- this preserves the Hebrew verbal construction (the "continuing" verb) and carries a subtly different weight than "anymore": it implies that YHWH's presence was an ongoing act of will that will now cease, rather than simply a temporal endpoint.

#### 7:13 -- Consecration command

**"Consecrate" vs. "sanctify":** EMET uses "sanctify" (קַדֵּשׁ); LOGOS and PNEUMA use "consecrate." Both are valid renderings of the Piel of קדשׁ. **Consensus adopts "consecrate"** (LOGOS, PNEUMA) -- it is the more commonly understood English term for the ritual preparation of a people before a divine encounter. "Sanctify" is equally accurate but slightly more archaic.

#### 7:14 -- The selection process; "takes" vs. "selects"

**"Takes" (יִלְכְּדֶנּוּ) vs. "selects":** EMET: "takes"; LOGOS: "takes"; PNEUMA: "selects." The verb לכד means "to capture, to take" -- the same verb used for capturing cities in warfare. **Consensus adopts "takes"** (EMET, LOGOS) -- LOGOS's note is insightful: the sacred lot "captures" the guilty party as a military operation captures an enemy. "Selects" (PNEUMA) softens this martial dimension.

**"Come near" vs. "come forward":** EMET: "shall-draw-near"; LOGOS: "shall come near"; PNEUMA: "will come forward." The Hebrew verb is קרב (to draw near/approach), the standard term for sacral approach. **Consensus adopts "shall come near"** (LOGOS) -- "come near" preserves the sacral-approach sense of קרב, while "come forward" (PNEUMA) is spatially accurate but loses the ritual connotation.

#### 7:15 -- Punishment; "disgraceful thing" (nevalah)

**"Disgraceful thing" vs. "outrage":** EMET: "a-disgraceful-thing"; LOGOS: "a disgraceful thing"; PNEUMA: "an outrage." The Hebrew נְבָלָה designates morally repulsive conduct that violates the community's fundamental norms. **Consensus adopts "a disgraceful thing"** (EMET, LOGOS) -- the term nevalah is a technical category in Hebrew law (Genesis 34:7; Judges 19:23-24; 2 Samuel 13:12), and "disgraceful thing" preserves the sense of a recognized category of offense. "Outrage" (PNEUMA) captures the emotional force but loses the categorical nature.

#### 7:16-18 -- The narrowing selection

All three agents agree in substance on these verses. **Consensus follows LOGOS's rendering** as the most balanced, with EMET's structural precision on the genealogy. The progression from tribe to clan to household to individual is preserved exactly.

#### 7:19 -- Joshua's adjuration; "give glory...give confession"

**"I pray" vs. "please" vs. "now":** EMET: "give, please, glory" and "tell, please"; LOGOS: "give glory, I pray" and "Tell me now"; PNEUMA: "give honor" and "Tell me." The Hebrew נָא (na') is the particle of polite entreaty. **Consensus adopts "I pray"** (LOGOS's first usage) for both occurrences of נָא -- "give, I pray, glory to YHWH" and "Tell me, I pray, what you have done." The rendering "I pray" preserves the solemn adjuration register that this verse requires: Joshua is not making a casual request but a formal sacred demand for truthful testimony.

**"Glory" vs. "honor":** EMET and LOGOS use "glory" (כָבוֹד); PNEUMA uses "honor." **Consensus adopts "glory"** -- the phrase "give glory to YHWH" is a fixed adjuration formula (cf. John 9:24), and "glory" preserves the formula's sacral weight.

**"Confession" (תוֹדָה):** All three agents agree on "confession." **Consensus retains "confession."**

#### 7:20 -- Achan's confession begins

**"Truly, I have sinned" vs. "It is true. I have sinned":** EMET: "Truly, I -- I have-sinned"; LOGOS: "Truly, I have sinned"; PNEUMA: "It is true. I have sinned." The Hebrew אָמְנָה אָנֹכִי חָטָאתִי uses the emphatic particle אָמְנָה ("truly/indeed") and the independent pronoun אָנֹכִי for emphasis. **Consensus adopts "Truly, I -- I have sinned"** (following EMET's structure) -- the em-dash isolating the emphatic pronoun preserves the Hebrew's double emphasis: the truth-particle + the independent pronoun (where the verb alone would have sufficed). "I -- I have sinned" captures the weight of Achan's self-identification as the guilty party.

#### 7:21 -- The spoil; Shinar; "tongue of gold"; the sin pattern

**"Mantle of Shinar" vs. "robe from Babylon":** EMET: "one-robe of-Shinar"; LOGOS: "a beautiful mantle of Shinar"; PNEUMA: "a beautiful robe from Babylon." **Consensus adopts "one beautiful mantle of Shinar"** -- "mantle" (LOGOS) is a more precise rendering of אַדֶּרֶת than "robe" (a mantle is a prestige garment/cloak). "Shinar" is retained over "Babylon" (PNEUMA) because it is the Hebrew text's geographic term, and it carries resonance with the Babel narrative (Genesis 11:2) that "Babylon" does not -- Shinar is the primeval name, connecting Achan's coveting to the deeper biblical narrative of human grasping.

**"Tongue of gold" vs. "bar of gold":** EMET: "one-tongue of-gold"; LOGOS: "one bar of gold"; PNEUMA: "a bar of gold." The Hebrew is לְשׁוֹן זָהָב -- literally "a tongue of gold," referring to a bar or wedge shaped like a tongue. **Consensus adopts "tongue of gold"** (following EMET) -- this is a distinctive Hebrew idiom that "bar" flattens. The object's shape (tongue-shaped) is what the Hebrew names, and the image is vivid and comprehensible in English.

**"One...one..." -- the numeral אַחַת/אֶחָד:** EMET preserves "one robe...one tongue." LOGOS and PNEUMA drop the numeral for the mantle. **Consensus preserves "one"** for both items -- the Hebrew אַחַת...אֶחָד emphasizes the specificity and singularity of each coveted item, and it creates a rhythmic inventory effect.

**The sin pattern "I saw...I coveted...I took":** All three agents note the echo of Genesis 3:6 (Eve: "she saw...it was desirable...she took"). LOGOS and PNEUMA provide extensive commentary. **Consensus preserves the three-verb sequence exactly** as it appears in the Hebrew: "I saw...I coveted them...I took them" (וָאֶרְאֶה...וָאֶחְמְדֵם...וָאֶקָּחֵם). The verb חמד ("coveted") is the same verb used in the Tenth Commandment (Exodus 20:17).

#### 7:22-23 -- Recovery of the stolen goods

**"Spread them out" vs. "poured them out":** EMET: "poured-them-out" (וַיַּצִּקֻם); LOGOS: "spread them out"; PNEUMA: "spread them out." The verb יצק means "to pour out." **Consensus adopts "spread them out"** (LOGOS, PNEUMA) -- while "poured out" is etymologically precise, the physical action described is the laying out of stolen goods before YHWH for display and judgment. "Spread them out" communicates this forensic display more clearly; "poured" suggests liquid, which is misleading for solid objects.

#### 7:24 -- The Valley of Achor; Achan's family

**Formatting of the inventory:** The long list of Achan, the stolen goods, his family, livestock, and possessions is handled variously. EMET uses commas throughout. LOGOS restructures with "Joshua, and all Israel with him, took Achan" -- placing the corporate agent first. PNEUMA uses em-dashes: "Joshua -- together with all Israel -- took Achan." **Consensus adopts the em-dash structure** (PNEUMA, modified): "And Joshua -- and all Israel with him -- took Achan" -- this preserves the Hebrew's "And-took Joshua...and-all Israel with-him" while using the em-dash to clarify the parenthetical "and all Israel with him" without losing it in a long comma chain.

#### 7:25 -- The wordplay; singular-to-plural shift; execution

**"Troubled/trouble" (עכר):** EMET: "troubled...will-trouble"; LOGOS: "troubled...will trouble"; PNEUMA: "brought this disaster...bring disaster." The Hebrew is a paronomastic judgment oracle: מֶה עֲכַרְתָּנוּ יַעְכֳּרְךָ יְהוָה -- the root עכר appears twice, binding crime to punishment through sound. **Consensus adopts "troubled...will trouble"** (EMET, LOGOS) -- the English verb "trouble" can be repeated to mirror the Hebrew repetition, preserving the wordplay. PNEUMA's "disaster" is interpretively stronger but loses the sound-echo that connects to the name Achor.

**Singular-to-plural shift:** The Hebrew shifts from "stoned him" (אֹתוֹ, singular) to "burned them" and "stoned them" (אֹתָם, plural). All three agents note this and preserve it. **Consensus preserves the shift exactly** as in the MT -- the singular may refer to Achan alone, the plural to all his possessions and family. The LXX reads plural throughout; the consensus follows the MT.

#### 7:26 -- The etiology; closure of divine anger

**"Unto this day" vs. "to this day" vs. "it is still there to this day":** EMET: "until this-day"; LOGOS: "unto this day"; PNEUMA: "it is still there to this day." **Consensus adopts "unto this day"** (LOGOS) -- the formula עַד הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה is an etiological marker that appears frequently in Joshua, and "unto this day" is its standard formal English equivalent, preserving the archival-witness tone of the formula.

**"Turned from the burning of His anger":** EMET: "turned-back YHWH from-the-burning of-His-anger"; LOGOS: "YHWH turned from the burning of his anger"; PNEUMA: "YHWH turned from the ferocity of his anger." The Hebrew is וַיָּשָׁב יְהוָה מֵחֲרוֹן אַפּוֹ -- literally "turned YHWH from the burning of his anger/nostril." **Consensus adopts "YHWH turned from the burning of His anger"** (LOGOS) -- "burning" is the literal rendering of חֲרוֹן; "ferocity" (PNEUMA) is interpretive.

**"Therefore" vs. "Upon-this":** EMET: "Upon-this"; LOGOS: "Therefore"; PNEUMA: "That is why." **Consensus adopts "Therefore"** (LOGOS) -- the Hebrew עַל־כֵּן is the standard causal connector, and "Therefore" is its established English equivalent.

---

### Key Wordplays and Literary Structures

#### 1. The Cherem Contagion

The word חֵרֶם (cherem, "devoted thing") appears in verses 1, 11, 12 (twice), 13 (twice), and 15, forming the theological backbone of the chapter. The concept functions as a contagion: Achan took from what was cherem; Israel therefore became cherem (v. 12). The contamination spreads from object to person to nation. Only the surgical removal of the contaminated element can restore the covenantal relationship. This contagion-logic explains the severity of the punishment and the inclusion of Achan's household in the judgment.

#### 2. The Achan/Achor/'Akhar Sound-Chain

The MT name Achan (עָכָן) resonates with the verb 'akhar (עָכַר, "to trouble") in verse 25 and the place-name Achor (עָכוֹר, "trouble") in verses 24 and 26. The LXX variant Achar (Αχαρ) makes the connection even more explicit. Joshua's pronouncement -- "Why have you troubled us? YHWH will trouble you" -- binds the man's identity to his crime through sound, and the valley's name memorializes the judgment permanently. The guilty man's name is inscribed in the landscape.

#### 3. The Genesis 3:6 Echo -- "I Saw...I Coveted...I Took"

Achan's confession (v. 21) follows the precise pattern of the primordial fall: Eve "saw that the tree was good...it was desirable...she took" (Genesis 3:6). The verbal sequence -- ראה (saw), חמד (coveted/desired), לקח (took) -- is the paradigmatic pattern of disobedience throughout Scripture. The verb חמד is the same verb prohibited in the Tenth Commandment (Exodus 20:17). Achan's sin is presented as a recapitulation of the fundamental human pattern: the eye desires, the heart covets, the hand takes what has been forbidden.

#### 4. The Heart-Melting Reversal (5:1 / 7:5)

In Joshua 5:1, the hearts of the Canaanite kings "melted" (מסס) upon hearing of Israel's crossing. In 7:5, the same verb is applied to Israel: "the heart of the people melted and became as water." The disobedience of one man has transferred the enemies' terror to Israel itself. The literary reversal is structural and deliberate, using the identical root to mark the inversion.

#### 5. The Fivefold Vegam (7:11)

YHWH's accusation stacks five verbs connected by וְגַם ("and also"): sinned, transgressed, taken, stolen, deceived. The repetition creates a relentless accumulation effect, as if YHWH is cataloguing each dimension of the offense. The chain progresses from general covenant violation (sinned, transgressed) to specific criminal acts (taken, stolen) to the compounding element of concealment (deceived, put among their vessels).

#### 6. The Inclusio of Wrath (7:1 / 7:26)

The chapter opens with YHWH's anger burning (וַיִּחַר אַף יְהוָה, v. 1) and closes with that anger turning away (וַיָּשָׁב יְהוָה מֵחֲרוֹן אַפּוֹ, v. 26). This forms an envelope structure: wrath ignited by sacrilege, wrath extinguished by judgment. The entire narrative between these poles -- defeat, lament, accusation, identification, confession, execution -- is the process by which the covenantal relationship moves from rupture to restoration.

#### 7. Joshua 6 and 7 -- Triumph and Disaster

The contrast between chapters 6 and 7 is stark and deliberate. The triumphant conquest of Jericho is immediately followed by humiliating defeat at the Ai. Israel's power was never autonomous; it depended entirely on the covenantal relationship with YHWH. One man's sacrilege nullified the military capacity of the entire nation. The adversative "But" that opens 7:1 marks the reversal.

---

### Textual Notes by Verse

#### 7:1 -- וַיִּמְעֲלוּ...מַעַל (cognate accusative)

The root מעל is a sacral term denoting breach of faith with what is holy -- not ordinary wrongdoing, but the specific violation of a sacred boundary. The cognate accusative construction (verb + cognate noun) is an intensifying device. The LXX adds μεγαλην ("great") as an interpretive expansion: "they committed a great offense." The consensus follows the MT without the LXX expansion.

#### 7:2 -- הָעַי (ha-'Ai, "the Ai")

The definite article is preserved in the consensus throughout the chapter. The name means "the ruin" or "the heap." The LXX renders Γαι without the article. The article's preservation is a minority choice among English translations but is consistent with the Hebrew morphology that all three agents acknowledge.

#### 7:5 -- הַשְּׁבָרִים (ha-Shebarim)

The term, with the definite article, may be a proper place name or a common noun meaning "the broken places" or "the quarries" (from שׁבר, "to break"). The LXX apparently read it as a verbal form: συντριψαν αυτους ("they crushed them"). The consensus follows the MT in treating it as a toponym with the article.

#### 7:7 -- אֲהָהּ אֲדֹנָי יְהוִה (Alas, my Lord YHWH)

The interjection אֲהָהּ is the solemn anguished lament form used in prophetic address. The combination Adonai YHWH is the address form used in Jeremiah 1:6; 4:10; Ezekiel 4:14; 9:8. Joshua's prayer echoes the Israelites' wilderness complaints (Exodus 14:11-12; Numbers 14:2-3), casting him momentarily as a despairing murmurer -- a characterization YHWH sharply corrects in verse 10.

#### 7:7 -- הַעֲבִיר הֶעֱבַרְתָּ (infinitive absolute)

The Hiphil infinitive absolute + finite verb expresses emphasis. The consensus renders with doubling per project rules: "brought bringing." This construction appears throughout the Hebrew Bible as an intensifying device (cf. Genesis 2:17, מוֹת תָּמוּת, "dying you shall die").

#### 7:9 -- שִׁמְךָ הַגָּדוֹל (Your great name)

Joshua's appeal to YHWH's reputation among the nations -- "name theology" -- has deep roots in the Mosaic tradition (Exodus 32:12; Numbers 14:13-16; Deuteronomy 9:28). The logic assumes that YHWH's identity is publicly bound to Israel's fate in the sight of the nations.

#### 7:11 -- Fivefold וְגַם (vegam, "and also")

The MT repeats וְגַם five times. The LXX follows the same structure. The consensus preserves each "and also" to reflect the rhetorical accumulation.

#### 7:12 -- כִּי הָיוּ לְחֵרֶם (for they have become cherem)

This is the theological crux. The LXX softens to οτι εγενηθη ενοχος ("because he has become guilty") -- replacing the terrifying identification of Israel with the cherem with a milder legal guilt. The consensus follows the MT's stark declaration.

#### 7:14 -- אֲשֶׁר יִלְכְּדֶנּוּ יְהוָה (which YHWH takes)

The verb לכד ("to capture, take") is the same verb used for capturing cities in warfare (cf. 6:20). The sacred lot "captures" the guilty party. The LXX uses a different verb: ον αν δειξη κυριος ("whom the Lord shall designate"). The means of selection -- Urim and Thummim, lots, or another sacral mechanism -- is not specified.

#### 7:15 -- נְבָלָה (nevalah, "disgraceful thing")

This term designates morally repulsive conduct that violates the fundamental norms of the covenant community. It is the same word used for Shechem's rape of Dinah (Genesis 34:7), the crime at Gibeah (Judges 19:23-24; 20:6, 10), and Amnon's assault on Tamar (2 Samuel 13:12). The word carries weight far beyond "foolishness."

#### 7:19 -- שִׂים כָּבוֹד...וְתֶן תוֹדָה (give glory...give confession)

The phrase "give glory to YHWH" is a solemn adjuration formula calling for truthful testimony (cf. John 9:24). Combined with "give a confession" (todah, from ידה), this is a formal legal-religious demand. The courtroom and the sanctuary converge.

#### 7:21 -- אַדֶּרֶת שִׁנְעָר (mantle of Shinar)

The addereth is a prestige garment. "Shinar" is the biblical name for Babylonia (Genesis 10:10; 11:2; 14:1, 9; Daniel 1:2). A Babylonian garment among the spoils of a Canaanite city reflects the international trade networks of the Late Bronze Age. The LXX omits the geographical reference to Shinar entirely, rendering "a beautiful decorated garment." The consensus preserves "Shinar" for its deep biblical resonance with the Babel narrative.

#### 7:21 -- לְשׁוֹן זָהָב (tongue of gold)

A bar or wedge of gold shaped like a tongue. The Hebrew idiom is distinctive and is preserved in the consensus over the flattened "bar of gold."

#### 7:21 -- וָאֶחְמְדֵם (I coveted them)

The verb חמד is the Tenth Commandment verb (Exodus 20:17; Deuteronomy 5:21). Its appearance in Achan's confession makes his sin a paradigmatic covenant violation.

#### 7:24 -- עֵמֶק עָכוֹר (Valley of Achor)

The name means "Valley of Trouble," from the root עכר. Hosea 2:17 [English 2:15] later promises that YHWH will transform the Valley of Achor into "a door of hope" -- a stunning eschatological reversal of this judgment scene.

#### 7:25 -- Singular then plural (אֹתוֹ / אֹתָם)

The shift from "stoned him" (singular, Achan) to "burned them...stoned them" (plural, all under the ban) follows the MT exactly. The LXX reads plural throughout. The shift may distinguish the execution of Achan from the destruction of his possessions and livestock, or may include the family. The consensus preserves the MT's alternation without harmonization.

#### 7:25 -- מֶה עֲכַרְתָּנוּ יַעְכֳּרְךָ יְהוָה (paronomasia)

The root עכר appears twice, creating a judgment oracle that binds crime to punishment through sound. The same root generates the valley's name (Achor) and resonates with Achan's name (especially in the variant Achar). The LXX uses a different root (ολεθρ-, "destroy") but preserves the repetition.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Comparison -- Joshua 7

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Consensus Decision |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7:1 | עָכָן (Achan) | Αχαρ (Achar) | "Achan" -- MT followed; LXX variant connects more directly to wordplay but is not the Leningrad reading |
| 7:1 | מַעַל (trespass) | πλημμελειαν μεγαλην (great offense) | "a trespass" -- MT followed; LXX expansion "great" not adopted |
| 7:2 | הָעַי (ha-'Ai, with article) | Γαι (without article) | "the Ai" -- MT article preserved |
| 7:5 | הַשְּׁבָרִים (the Shebarim, toponym) | συντριψαν αυτους (they crushed them, verb) | "the Shebarim" -- MT toponym followed; LXX reads as verb |
| 7:12 | הָיוּ לְחֵרֶם (became a devoted thing) | εγενηθη ενοχος (became guilty) | "become a devoted thing" -- MT's stark identification preserved; LXX softening not adopted |
| 7:14 | יִלְכְּדֶנּוּ (takes/captures) | δειξη (designates) | "takes" -- MT martial verb preserved; LXX's milder "designates" not adopted |
| 7:21 | אַדֶּרֶת שִׁנְעָר (mantle of Shinar) | ψιλην ποικιλην καλην (beautiful decorated garment) | "mantle of Shinar" -- MT geographical term preserved; LXX omission of Shinar noted |
| 7:25 | אֹתוֹ...אֹתָם (him...them, singular-to-plural) | plural throughout | Singular-to-plural preserved following MT |
